source: Claims against Malayan Government. British Overseas Airways Corporation__CO273:670:3
filename: Claims against Malayan Government. British Overseas Airways Corporation__CO273:670:3.txt
summary:
- **Core topic/purpose:** Colonial Office file on **claims submitted by British Overseas Airways Corporation (B.O.A.C.)** for reimbursement of **air passages, freight, and carriage of diplomatic mail** connected with Malayan/Straits Settlements authorities, many left unpaid due to the **fall/evacuation of Singapore**.  
- **Geography/time period:** Concerns **Singapore and Malaya (Straits Settlements)** with routing via **Cairo/London** and **Australia (Qantas)**; main correspondence and internal minutes run **1942–1943** (with underlying services dated **1941–Feb 1942**).  
- **Major decisions/proposals/events:** Initial Colonial Office stance (Oct 1942) was **not to accept liability for local Straits Settlements debts** until **re-occupation of Malaya** due to lack of records; later (1943) the Office moved toward **settling certain claims** provided **evidence** and an **indemnity/refund undertaking** were supplied to protect against duplicate/overpayment.  
- **Key actors/organizations:** **Colonial Office** (notably J. J. Paskin; B. Foster-Hall; Mr. Caine and others), **B.O.A.C. Funds Accountant/Legal Branch**, **Messrs. Mansfield & Co. Ltd. (Singapore)** as BOAC’s local agents, **Qantas Empire Airways** (Australia), **Air Ministry** (Civil Aviation; H.E. Gregory), **Ministry of War Transport**, **Trading with the Enemy Department (Treasury/Board of Trade)**, **Treasury** and **Custodian of Enemy Property** referenced.  
- **Trading-with-the-enemy/legal issue:** Extensive discussion (1943) of whether payments could safely be made where intermediaries/agents or companies were **incorporated in Japanese-occupied territory**; references include the **House of Lords “Waalhaven” case** and guidance that enemy-company debts may need payment to the **Custodian** or require careful determination of “enemy” status and valid authority/receipt.  
- **Notable claims and amounts (examples):** Diplomatic mail charges include **£6/1** (1 bag) and **£40.16.2** (5 bags) mentioned as outstanding; an invoice for **Cairo–London diplomatic mail** shows **£4.8.5** (Invoice 6856, dated **9.9.42**) and another for **5 bags** (Invoice 6857, **9.9.42**). Other schedules list larger items such as **freight on jewellery to Australia £A354.17.10**, **freight on mail bags £A31.18.3**, and **passages (staff) £A1930.4.8** (as summarized in April 1943 minutes).  
- **Specific passage liability example:** **Sir Alexander Small’s** passage **Singapore–Calcutta** was treated as payable by the **Colonial Office Vote (Sub-head “B”)**; BOAC acknowledged receipt of **£47.10.0** in settlement (noted in BOAC letter **14.11.42**).
